CAPEVILLE, a village of Northampton co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 170 m. S of Richmond, on the E shore of Chesapeake bay, near Cape Charles.

CAPE VINCENT, or GNIVELLY POINT, a village of Lyme township, Jefferson co., in the state of New York, U. S., 190 m. NW of Albany, on the S side of St. Lawrence river, opposite Kingston, Canada, with which it has regular communication.
CAPHEATON, a township in the p. of Kirkwhelpington, Northumberland, 4 m. NE of Hexham. Pop. in 1841, 213.

CAPIBARI, a small town of Brazil, in the prov. and 75 m. W of Sao-Paulo, on a river of the same name. Pop. 2,000. The construction of boats and canoes, and the distillation of rum, locally called cachaca, form the chief employments of the inhabitants. The river C. unites with the Tiete, 15 m. below Porto Felix. Its banks abound with timber of large dimensions.—Also a river of the prov. of Santa Catharina, which unites with the Tubarao, 5 m. above the entrance of that river into the sea. It is navigable about 20 m.—Also a river of the prov. of Sao-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, which issues from a lake at the foot of the Serra-Geral, waters the parishes of Santo Antonio and Arroyo, and falls into the Sino.—Also a lake in the same province, in the sandy tract, between the lake of Potos, into which it discharges itself, and the ocean.—Also a small river in the same prov., in the sandy tract of the Torres. It forms the outlet of lake Barros, and falls into the Jacui, near lake Viamao.—Also a tributary of the Ivahi, in the prov. of Sao-Paulo.

CAPIBARIBE, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, which takes its rise in the Serra dos Cairiris-Velhos, and runs E and ESE, in a circuitous course, through a rocky channel for 150 m., when it divides into two unequal branches, but both navigable; the smaller bends to the SE and falls into the ocean, its estuary forming the port of Affogados; the larger continues its course to the ESE, and uniting with the Biberibe, enters the Atlantic near Recife.

CAPIBARIBE-MIRIM, a small river of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, district of Goyanna, which, after the junction of the Tracunhaim, becomes the Goyanna.—Also a small river in the prov. of Sao- Paulo, which falls into the Tiete.
CAPILLA, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in Foggia, Bovino, and San Severo; which are subdi- CAP of the Zujar.
CAPILLAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 21 m. N of Valladolid, on the Canal de Campos.
CAPIM, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, which, after a course of about 250 m. from SSW to NNE, flows into the Guama, on the l. bank, 35 m. E of Para.

CAPIMESCAW (LAKE), one of an extensive chain of lakes in the Rupert's river district of Labrador, which discharge their waters into James's bay by the Slade river. It is intersected by the meridian of 70° W, and the parallel of 52° 15' N.

CAPIRA, a small river of Venezuela, flowing into the Caribbean sea, 60 m. W of Barcelona, after a course of about 50 m. from SE to NW.-There is a small town of the same name on its r. bank.—Also a town on a small river of the same name, in New Grenada, in the prov. of Ystmo, 25 m. SW of Panama.

CAPIS, or Capiz, a district and town in the island of Panay, in the Philippine group.—The district embraces the N part of the island, with the islets lying off the coast. It is fertile in rice; and produces dye-woods and gold dust. Its pop. in 1837 was estimated at 120,520.—The town is at the mouth of a small river, in N lat. 11° 25'.
CAPISTRANO, a village of Naples, in Calabria Ultra, in the cant. of Monterosso, in a deep gorge of the Apennines, 20 m. NE of Squillace. Pop. 1,000.
CAPISTRELLO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo Ultra 2da, 7 m. SSW of Avezzano. Pop. 1,300.

CAPITANA'TA, an administrative division of the kingdom of Naples, comprising the group of the Tremiti islands, and the island of Pianosa. It is bounded on the N and E by the gulf of Venice; on the SE by the prov. of Bari; on the S by Basilicata and Principato Ultra; and on the W by Sannio. Its area is 2,359 Neapolitan sq. m., or 2,387,780 moggia, the moggia being less than an English acre in the proportion of 1 to 1.2106. On the W and N, this region is covered with mountain-ranges connected with the Apennines, which on the E coast, in Monte Gargano, form the peninsular projection of that name, sometimes known as the 'Spur' of Italy. The S and E districts generally present a low sandy plain, known as the Tavoliere di Puglia.—The principal rivers are the Fortore, which falls into the sea on the N coast; and the Candelaro, the Cervaro, the Carapella, and the Ofanto, which have their mouths on the E coast. The latter river divides this prov. from those of Basilicata and Bari. The coasts, except in the vicinity of Monte Gargano, are generally low, and bordered by saline lakes or lagunes, of which those of Lesina and Varano on the N coast, and Pantano- Salso and Salpi, on the E, at the bottom of the gulf of Manfredonia, are the principal.—About one-sixth of the surface is covered with forests; and at least two-thirds of the Tavoliere are occupied with natural pasture, on which immense herds of cattle, and large flocks of sheep, collected from the neighbouring provinces, find winter-pasturage. Corn is not extensively cultivated. The wines of San-Giovanni, Vico, and Manfredonia, are esteemed; and the olive-oil and manna of the Gargano. A small quantity of tobacco is grown, and a little silk is produced; but the great source of industrial employment is the rearing of cattle, sheep, and horses. Cheese is extensively made for export; and the lagunes furnish salt and eels for exportation.-The cap. and principal entrepot is Foggia, 78 m. ENE of Naples. There are small ports at Manfredonia, Rodi, and Viesti. The number of merchant vessels belonging to these ports in 1834 was 57 = 1028 tons.—The prov. is divided into the 3 distretto or districts of vided into 23 circondario, and 62 comune. - Pop. | in 1820, 266,347; in 1837, 302,666; in 1845, 311,517.
CAPITANEJO, a town of New Grenada, 105 m. NE of Tunja, near the r. bank of the Gallinazos, at the foot of the mountain-range of Lomas del Viento.
CAPITCHA, a small town of Turkey, in the pashalik of Trikala, 7 m. S of Caritza.
CAPIVARAS, the name of several islands of the Mamore in Brazil, in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, in S lat. 11° 14'.

CAPIVARI, a town and parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, comarca of Cabo-Frio. Pop. 3,000. Agriculture and the preparation of timber for export form the chief branches of local industry.—Also a mountain of the same prov., to the E of the Serra di Santa Anna, and which forms the S extremity of the Cordelheira dos Aimores. It gives rise to a small river of the same name, which runs SE, passes the new town of Capivari, and falls into Lake Juturnahiba.—Also a parish in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, district and 5 m. S of Pouzo-Alto. -Also a small river of the prov. of Matto-Grosso, which falls into the Guapore, 13 m. above Matto- Grosso. - Also a small river of the prov. of Goyaz, a tributary of the Corumba.

CAPIZZI, a town of Sicily, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. and 75 m. WSW of Messina, district and 11 m. SE of Mistretta, on the S side of the Neptunian mountains. Pop. 3,488. Marble, petroleum, asphalte, and iron, are found in the vicinity.
CAPLE, a parish of Kent, 3 m. SE of Tonbridge, near the SE railway. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. in 1841, 516.
CAPO-MESURADO. See LIBERIA.
CAPO-DI-MONTE. See Napoli.

CAPO-DI-PONTE, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, delegation and 45 m. NE of Bergamo, in the Val Camonica, on the l. bank of the Oglio, at an alt. of 1,374 ft. above sea-level. CAPO-DI-SOPRA, CAPO-DI-SASSARI, or CAP.- Superieur, the more southerly of the two governments into which the island of Sardinia is divided. It comprises 5 prov.: viz., Sassari, Alghero, Cuglieri, Ozieri, and Tempio. The cap. is Sassari. CAPO-DI-SOTTO, CAPO-DI-CAGLIERI, OF CAP.-

INFERIEUR, the more northerly of the two governments into which the island of Sardinia is divided. It comprises 6 prov.: viz., Cagliari, Iglesias, Isili, Lanusei, Nuoro, and Busachi. Its cap. is Caglieri.

CAPO D'ISTRIA, a port of the Austrian province of Illyria, in the circle of Pisino, on the Adriatic, in N lat. 45° 32′ 42″, E long. 13° 44′ 1″. It is built upon an oval-shaped rock surrounded by the sea, and connected with the mainland by a long stone causeway. Its chief export-trade is in salt, oil, and wines; its manufactures are leather, soap, and candles. Pop. 6,000. It has a fine cathedral, and is one of the seats of the united bishopric of Trieste-et-Capo d'Istria.—It was the Ægida of the Romans, and the Justinopolis of the Western empire; and in later times the cap. of Venetian Istria.
CAPODRISE, a village of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavora, district and 11 m. SW of Caserta. Pop. 2,415. It contains a handsome church, and an hospital.
CAPOLEQUI, a town of Peru, in the Pampas del Sacramento, on the l. bank of the Iguatiqui, an affluent of the Pachitea, and 220 m. SSE of the junction of the latter river with the Paro.

CAPOLIVERE, a canton and v. of the island of Elba, 5 m. SSE of Porto-Ferrajo, and 2 m. SW of Porto-Longone. Pop. of the cant. 3,000. The situation of the v. commands an extensive view of the coast. CAP
CAPOLONA, a village of Tuscany, cap. of a communita, 9 m. NW of Arezzo. Pop. 447.
CAPOMAZZO, a volcanic hill on the coast of the bay of Naples adjoining Monte Barbaro. It is composed of light pumiceous conglomerate, and has a well-defined crater.
CAPONES (CAPE), a promontory of the island of Luzon, in the Asiatic archipelago, on the E coast, in N lat. 15° 54'. In the vicinity are two small inhabited islands of the same name.
CAPOOEY, a small river of British Guayana, which falls into the Atlantic to the N of the Essequibo.

CAPOONACAUGANY, a river of Upper Canada, which flows into an inosculation of the Albany, 200 m. SW of the entrance of that river into James bay. CAPORETTO, Caporoto, or Karfried, a town of Austria, in Illyria, in the prov. of Trieste, circle and 20 m. N of Gorizia, on the r. bank of the Isonzo.
CAPOSELE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Principato-Citra, district and 15 m. NNE of Campagna, near the source of the Sele. Pop. 4,480. Fairs are held here twice a-year.
CAPOSOS, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, on the Todos-os-Santos, and near the Serra das Safiras. It is inhabited by aborigines of the same name.

CAPOT, a river of the island of Martinique, which takes its rise near Morne - Calebasse, runs N, receiving on the l. the Grand-Falaise, and separating the parishes of La Basse-Pointe and La Grand-Anse, and after a total course of 8 m., falls into the sea 3 m. ESE of Basse-Point.—Another river, named the Petit Capot, rises in Mont Pelee, and enters the sea about 1½ m. NW of the preceding.
CAPOTERRA, a village in the island of Sardinia, 2½ hours from Cagliari. Pop. 800.
CAPPACLOUGH, a village in the p. of Kilgobbin, co. Kerry, on the road from Tralee to Dingle. Pop. 277.

CAPPADOCIA, or CAPADOCIA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo Ultra, district and 10 m. WSW of Avezzano, and 25 m. S of Aquila, near the source of the Lira. Pop. 1,771. It contains a custom-house.

CAPPAGH, a parish and v. in co. Limerick, 24 m. NNE of Rathkeale. Area of p. 1,268 acres. Pop. 755. Pop. of v. 108.—Also a p. in co. Tyrone, 21 m. N of Omagh. Area 37,669 acres. Pop. 13,330. In Mullaghcarn, 5 m. NE of Omagh, the surface of this p. attains an alt. of 1,778 ft. above sea-level.— Also a rivulet in co. Galway, joining the Ballyshrule, after an E course of 7 m., and flowing half-a-mile lower down into the bay of Gloongagauve.
CAPPAGHWAITE, a small post-town in the p. of Toem, co. Tipperary, 7 m. N of Tipperary. Pop. in 1831, 695; in 1841, 1,046.
CAPPAMORE, or Tough, a village in the p. of Tough, co. Limerick, 4 m. N by E of Pallasgreen. Pop. 551.
CAPPANACUSHY, a group of islets in the p. of Templenoe, co. Kerry, near the head of the Kenmare estuary.

CAPPEL, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 11 m. SSW of Zurich, bailiwick and 1 m. E of Knonau, on the road to Zug, and on the W side of the Albis mountains, is celebrated as the scene of a sanguinary battle between the Catholic and Protestant parties, which took place on the 3d October, 1531. In the vicinity are the baths of Wenghi.— Also a village of the cant. of St. Gallen, circle of Upper Toggenburg, on the r. bank of the Thur, 4 m. SSE of Lichtenstego.
CAPPELEN, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. and 6 m. W of Berne, near the l. bank of the Aar.

CAPPELN, or KAPPELN, a town of Hanover, in the circle and 9 m. NE of Osnabruck.-Also a town of ] Prussia, in the prov. of Westphalia, regency and 26 m. NNE of Munster, and 8 m. WNW of Osnaburg. These towns are commonly distinguished by their relative positions as Oster and Wester C.—Also a town of Denmark, in the duchy and 15 m. NE of Sleswig, on the Schley.
CAPPOG, a parish in co. Louth, 1 m. N by W of Dunleer. Area 1,284 acres. Pop. 568.

CAPPOQUIN, a town in the p. of Lismore, in co. Waterford, 23 m. E by N of Lismore, on an angular bend of the Blackwater, which is navigable to this part for barges. Pop. in 1831, 2,289; in 1841, 2,341.
CAPRACOTTA, a town of Naples, cap. of a circle in the prov. of Sannio, district and 20 m. NNE of Isernia. Pop. 2,860. It has annual fairs.
CAPRADOSSO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, district and 10 m. SE of Civita-Ducale. Pop. 1,009. It contains a custom-house.

CAPRAJA, a volcanic island of the Mediterranean, 20 m. E of Cape Corso, and about the same distance NW of the island of Elba, in N lat. 43° 5', E long. 9° 48'. It is about 15 m. in circumference, comprising an area of 7 Italian sq. m., and contains 2,500 inhabitants, chiefly fishermen. It generally is mountainous, and, except on the E coast-on which the town of the same name and port are situated-difficult of access. The port is safe and commodious, and is defended by a fort. Wine forms the chief article of local produce. The mountains still abound with wild goats. In 1507, this island, the Capraria and Ægilon of the ancients, was taken from Corsica by the Genevese; and it now forms a part of the prov. of Genoa.—Also a village of Tuscany, the ancient Capraria, cap. of a com. in the comp. and 15 m. W of Florence, at the junction of the Pesa with the Arno. Pop. 877. It possesses some pottery manufactories.
CAPRANICA, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the delegation and 10 m. SSE of Viterbo, and 3 m. S of Lake Vico.
CAPRANU, or CAPRENA, a village of Greece, on the W side of the plain of Chæronea, 6 m. N of Livadia, on the site of the ancient Chæronea.

CAPRARA, an uninhabited island of the Adriatic, in the Tremiti group, in N lat. 42° 8', E long. 15° 33'. It is about <math>1\frac{1}{2}</math> m. in length, and <math>\frac{3}{4}</math> m. in breadth.
CAPRARUOLA, a town of the States-of-the- Church, in the delegation and 9 m. SE of Viterbo, and 3 m. NE of Lake Vico. It contains a magnificent castle, built by Vignole in the 16th century.
CAPRAZOPPO, a bare rocky cape in the Cornice, or maritime pass between Nice and Genoa. It separates the bay of Albenga from that of Finale.
CAPREO, a mountain-summit in the States-ofthe-Church, in the delegation and 17 m. WSW of Frosinone, between the Sacco and Pontine marshes. It rises to the height of 5,434 ft. above sea-level.

CAPRERA, an island of the Mediterranean, near the NE coast of the island of Sardinia, in N lat. 41° 12' 47", E long. 9° 28'. It is 6 m. in length, and 1\frac{1}{2} m. in breadth, and affords grain and excellent pasturage.
CAPRESE, a village of Tuscany, in the comp. and 21 m. NE of Arezzo. Pop. 200. It was the birthplace of Michael Angelo, in 1474.

CAPRI, the ancient Caprece, an island of the Mediterranean, situated on the S side of the bay of Naples, and separated by a narrow channel of 3 m. width from Cape Campanella - the Promontorium Athenœum, or Minervæ of the ancients—the extremity of the tongue of land which forms the S boundary of the bay, and separates it from the gulf of Salerno. The island, which is of a triangular form, is about 8 m. in circumf.; and is distant from the coast of Na- BRITARE PROPERTURATE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE SERVICE S CAP ples about 3 Italian m. It is composed of two masses of hard calcareous rock, or "unstratified Apennine limestone" [Forbes], of which the W is the highest, attaining an alt. of 1,600 ft. Its appearance at a distance is extremely rugged, from its steep cliffs and huge masses of rock; but this wildness is softened down, upon a nearer approach, by the appearance of patches of verdure and clusters of white houses. A great part of the island is uncultivated, and incapable of cultivation; but wherever the hoe can be used, the ground is well-tilled, and produces rich crops. The strip of land between the two rocky and elevated portions of the island is covered with fig-trees, almond-trees, oranges, olives, myrtles, and vines. Near the palace of La Marina, the soil, which is extremely rich, consists of a deep stratum of mould over a yellow bole, which is a stone similar to the tufa of the volcanic hills around Naples. The upper rocks of C., as we learn from Swinburne, are universally calcareous, and the strata accord with those of the Sorrentine mountains on the continent, from which the island appears to have been separated by some great convulsion. Mr. Swinburne supposes, that "the lower tracts of land in C. have been thrown up by fire in the midst of limestone mountains, in the same manner as the plain of Sorrento." The district of Anacapri, or the W division of the island, slopes to the N, and is bounded by a high and rocky shore. The communication between this and the other division of the island is kept up by a flight of 538 rude steps, winding up a cliff of tremendous height; the steps are slippery, yet loaded asses are able to ascend and descend them with safety. The S and E sides of the island are flanked with rocky precipices of enormous height.—The principal places in the island are Capri and Anacapri. Capri is situated in a low but fertile spot of land, between two rugged eminences of great height which form the E extremities of the island. At a distance its cupolas and buildings give it the appearance of a considerable place, though it is only a small village.—Anacapri is a small town, composed of a few streets and scattered houses, with a nunnery and a parish-church, embosomed in groves of fruit-trees, and encircled with luxuriant and wellkept gardens.—At the chapel of Santa Maria, near the E extremity of the island, stood the summerpalace of Tiberius, who spent the greater part of ten years in this delightful retreat, abandoned to the most unhallowed debaucheries. Extensive vaults and reservoirs at Santa Maria, the ruins of a lighthouse, and two broken columns, are almost the only remains of the ancient villas. The winter-residence of Tiberius at La Marina is still indicated by columns and fragments scattered on the sand. The conduit from which the palace was supplied with water is still to be seen; and Mr. Swinburne is of opinion that a semicircular recess of network—the opus reticulatum of Vitruvius-which is raised against the cliff, once formed a part of the theatre. "In an adjacent vineyard," says that traveller, "some peasants were removing a pavement of black and white mosaic. The ruins stretch far into the sea, and that element has now resumed the territory from which it had formerly been expelled by the force of terraces and piers. The palace was built of tufa; but in its coarser parts, such as abutments and back-walls, are inserted large pieces of lava in a rough state of torrefaction like that of the crust of Vesuvian streams when cooled."—On the S shore, the Carthusians have a very spacious convent founded in the reign of King Robert, by James Capri, a veteran commander. Terraces supported by vaults, called botteghe, the shops of the ancients, are cut in the opposite hill; and tufts of caper bushes, laden with purple and white flowers, mingle happily with the ruined arcades.—The island of C. abounds with various birds of passage, but particularly with quails. The greater part of the bishop's income was said to be derived from the taking of these, and of other kinds of game; hence the island has been called 'the bishopric of Quails.' The see is now united to that of Sorrento. "Across every track in the woods," says Swinburne, "or chasm in the hills, rows of nets are placed to intercept stock-doves and quails in their annual flights; the quantity taken of each sort, especially the latter, is almost incredible. I have the best authority for saying, that even in bad years the number of quails caught in C. amounts to 12,000; in good years it exceeds 60,000; and in one remarkable year, 160,000 were netted. Eight years ago, in the month of May, 45,000 were taken in the course of a single day." The hills are also well-stocked with the red-legged partridge. The wines of the island are much esteemed. The pop. is estimated at 3,300 .- Swinburne's Travels in the Two Sicilies, 2d edit. vol. iii. p. 1-42.-Nuova Guida de Forestieri per l'antichita Curiosissime di Pozzuoli, dell' isole adjacenti d'Ischia, Procida, Nicida, Capri, &c. Naples, 1751, 12mo.—Sir R. C. Hoare's Classical Tour. Lond. 1829, 2 vols. 8vo.

CAPRI, a small town, the cap. of the above island, in the district of Castellamare. Pop. 1,843. It is well fortified; and has a fine castle, and a cathedral .- Also an island of Dalmatia, 9 m. W by S of the town of Sebenico on the mainland.
CAPRI (CAPE), a promontory on the SE coast of the island of Cephalonia, in N lat. 38° 8'.
CAPRI RIPALTA. See RIPALTA.
CAPRIATA, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 7 m. SW of Novi, near the r. bank of the Orba.
CAPRIATI, a town of Naples, in the Terra di Lavoro, 5 m. E of Venafro. Pop. 1,389.

CAPRICORN (CAPE), a headland on the NE coast of Australia, in S lat. 23° 30′ 30″ [Stokes]. It was discovered and named by Captain Cook, who supposed it to be directly under the tropic of C. It is white and barren, and of considerable height; and has a group of islands, called the C. islands, a little to the NW of it.
CAPRINO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, 10 m. WNW of Bergamo, near the l. bank of the Adda. Pop. 1,000.—Also a town in the gov. of Venice, 14 m. NW of Verona.
CAPRIO, a village of Tuscany, in the comp. of Pisa, 4 m. SE of Pontremoli. Pop. 328.

CAPRIOLO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. and 16 m. NW of Brescia, on the l. bank of the Oglio, near its issue from Lake Iseo. Pop. 1,500. It is an ancient town, and was formerly fortified.
CAPRONA, a village of Tuscany, in the comp. and 4 m. E of Pisa, on the r. bank of the Arno. Pop. 452. In the environs is a villa of the grandduke, built by Cosmo I.
CAPRYCKE, a commune and town of Belgium, in E Flanders, arrond. and 12 m. NW of Gand. Pop. 4,083.
CAPSALI, a town of the island of Cerigo, on the S coast, near Cape Trachilo.

CAPSIR, formerly a district of France, now comprised in the dep. of the Pyrenees-Orientales. It consists of an elevated and except at three points inaccessible valley, 12 m. in length and 9 in breadth.
CAPTAINGUNGE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 12 m. E of Oude.

CAPTIEUX, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Gironde, arrond. of Bazas. The cant. comprises 5 com., and in 1831 contained 3,612 inhabitants. The town is 11 m. S of Bazas, and 45 m. SSE of Bordeaux. Pop. 1,970. ~ CAR

CAPUA, or Capoa, a town of Naples, in the Terra di Lavoro, situated on the l. bank of the Volturno, "a river now, as formerly, rapid, muddy, and shallow," 12 m. above its embouchure, in a beautiful and fertile tract, at the foot of a mountain, 18 m. NW of Naples, in N lat. 41° 7'. The ancient town. celebrated in history as the place where the hardy soldiers of Hannibal allowed themselves to sink into effeminacy after their victory at Cannæ, is nearly 3 m. SE of the present town, on the spot now occupied by Santa Maria di C. The modern town, which occupies the site of the ancient Casilinum, is the seat of an archbishop, and of a royal marine academy founded in 1751; and has a pop. of 8,685. It is well-built; has 3 principal streets; and contains, besides the cathedral, a collegiate church, several parish-churches, and 12 convents. Simond pronounces it to be "an ugly dirty place, noisy beyond all bearing, and quite different from the ideas awakened by its name;" and Eustace confesses that "its greatest recommendation is its name." No place in Italy, with the exception of Rome, contains a greater number of ancient inscriptions and architectural fragments, which were no doubt brought hither from the old town.—C. was founded about the middle of the 9th cent. by the count and the bishop of C., was fortified by Vauban, and is accounted the key of Naples on the north. During the middle ages it was repeatedly plundered. In January 1799, it was occupied by a body of French troops, who were compelled to surrender themselves prisoners of war to a body of troops of various nations commanded by Captain Troubridge, of the British navy. In 1803, C. was greatly damaged by an earthquake.
CAPUANA, a village on the E shore of Lake Como, near the intermittent stream called, from its milky colour, the Fiume di Latti.
CAPUICUI LAKE, a lake of Ecuador, which discharges itself into the Napo 10 m. SE of the confluence of the Coca with that river.
CAPUL, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Philippine group, between the islands of Luzon and Samar, in N lat. 13° 30', E long. 124° 8'. It is about 9 m. in circumference, and very fertile.
CAPULA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 110 m. NNE of Mexico, and 30 m. ESE of Actopan.
CAPUL-ANGARY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malabar, 42 m. SE of Calicut, and about the same distance W of Coimbatoor.
CAPURSO, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. and 5 m. SSE of Bari. Pop. 3,160. Almonds are extensively cultivated in the environs.
CAPUR-SUNGUM, a village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bijapur and district of Nurgul, near the Sungum or confluence of the Malpurba with the Krishna.

CAPUTH, a parish in Perthshire, in the district of Stormont, comprehending an extensive portion of the vale of Strathmore, and watered by the Isla and the Tay. Area 16,000 acres. Pop. in 1801, 2,097; in 1841, 2,317.
CAPVERN, or Capbern, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrénees, cant. of Lannemezan, 8 m. ENE of Bagnères. Pop. 741.
CAQUEIRADA, an island of Brazil, in the bay of Nitherohi, between the city of Rio-de-Janeiro and the isle of Governador.
CAQUETA. See JAPURA.
CARA, a town of China, in the prov. of Petcheli, 90 m. NE of Pekin, on the Chantung river.
CARA, an island of Argyleshire, 31 m. W of Kintyre, and 1 m. S of Gigha. It is about 1 m. in length, and 1 m. in breadth. CARA. For many geographical names compounded with this word, see KARA.
CARABAILLO, a river of Bolivia, in the dist. of Cercado, by which 3 lagunes discharge themselves into the sea near Point Merques.
CARABALLEDA, a town of Venezuela, 11 m. N of Caracas, on the coast of the Caribbean sea.
CARABANDELLA, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, in the district of Araxa.
CARABAYA, a district on the confines of Peru and Bolivia, between the river Inambari on the E, and the Cordillera da Vilcanota on the W, intersected by the parallel of 14° 30' S.

CARABOBO, a province of Venezuela, bounded on the N by the prov. of Coro and the Caribbean sea; on the E by the prov. of Caracas; on the S by Varinas; and on the W by Truxillo. Area 8,148 sq. m. Pop. in 1837, 96,967. It is intersected by the head-streams of the Rio Portugueza, and comprises the greater part of the valley of Aragua. Wheat, coffee, indigo, tobacco, sugar, cacao, and cotton are its chief products. Its cap. is Valencia, the port of which is Puerta Cabella; but the prov. takes its name from the village of C., about 20 m. SW of Valencia, where a battle fought on June 24, 1821, won the independence of Colombia.
CARABELAS, or CARAVELAS, a port on the N coast of the island of Cuba, in W long. 77° 22'.

CARACA, a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, in 20° 6' S lat., and to the N of Marianna. It forms one of the highest summits of the Cordilheira da Mantequeira, and is remarkable for the similarity of its outline to that of a colossal human form, and for the circular shape of its valleys. The latter are of great fertility, producing fruit, grain, &c., and in one is a monastery, with a fine church and a college. In the centre of the ridge is a ferruginous spring.—Also a town in the prov. of Parahiba, at the junction of several roads, one of which runs through the pass of Embuzeiro.

CARACA, or CARRACA (LA), an island of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 6 m. SE of Cadiz, on the SE side of the bay of that name, and separated from the continent by the Santi Petri and the brook Culebras. It contains a town of the same name, and is one of the most important naval arsenals of Spain. It has three large careening basins, extensive building-docks, manufactories of sails, cordage, &c., reckoned the finest in Europe, barracks, an hospital, a church, &c. This establishment was founded by Charles III. It takes its name from the caraccas, carracks, or heavy ships of burden, of which it was originally the station.
CARACARA (POINT), a headland on the SW coast of Sumatra, in N lat. 0° 5'.

CARA'CAS, or CARACCAS, an old captain-generalship of Spanish S America; bounded on the N by the Caribbean sea, from the Cape de la Vela to the point of Paria; on the E by the sea, from the 12th to the 8th degree of N lat.; on the S by Dutch Guayana and Peru; and on the W by the kingdom of Santa Fé. It comprehended the prov. of Venezuela in the centre; the gov. of Maracaibo on the W; Guayana on the S; the gov. of Cumana on the E; and the island of Margaritta on the NE. This political division of S America no longer exists; but the larger portion of it is comprised in the republic of Venezuela; and the modern prov. of C. in that state extends along the coast from Punta de Aroa on the W to the Rio Suata on the E; and S to the banks of the Apure and the Orinoco. It lies between the parallels of 7° 38' and 10° 40' N; and between 2° 8' E of the city of C. to 57° W of C., comprising the valleys of the Aragua, the Tui, and the Guayra. Its area is estimated at 33,948 sq. m., or, according to Codazzi, 34,104 sq. m.; while the pop. in 1837 was 242,888, or 7.1 to a sq. m.; the average pop. of Venezuela being at the rate of 2.3 to a sq. m. The cantons are: Carácas, La Guayra, Petave, Guarénas, Caucagua, Rio Chico, Santa Lucia, Ocumare, La Victoria, Turmero, Maracai, Cura, San Sebastian, Orltuco, Chaguaramas, and Calabozo. [Geografia de Venezuela. Por Aug. Codazzi. Paris 1841.] The northern and most cultivated districts of this prov. are altogether mountainous, being covered with the great coast-chain and subordinate chains, which enclose several fertile longitudinal valleys, the most important of which is that of Aragua, which produces indigo, sugar, cotton, and European wheat. The S margin of this valley adjoins the lake of Tacarigua or Valencia, the height of which above sea-level is about 1,470 ft. less than the mean height of the valley of C. The llanos of C. are occupied by a great formation of conglomerate. Descending from the valley of Aragua, and crossing the most southern ridge of the coast-chain towards Parapara, we successively meet with gneiss and mica-slate, clay-slate and black limestone, serpentine and greenstone in detached masses, and lastly, on the margin of the great plain which extends southwards to the forests of Guayana, small elevations of amygdaloid and porphyritic slate. The elevation of the llanos at Calabozo, 400 m. from the sea, is only 30 toises or 192 ft. [Humboldt.] The city of Caracas, Santiago DE LEON DE

CARACAS, or more commonly CARACCAS, the capital of the old Spanish captain-generalship of the Caracas, now that of the republic of Venezuela, in N lat. 10° 30′ 15", W long. 67° 4′ 45". It is situated 560 m. NE of Bogota, and about 12 m. SSE from its port La Guayra, near the W extremity of the plain of Chacao, in a valley formed by that vast chain of mountains which coasts the sea from Coro to Cumana, and which extends nearly E and W about 12 m., and varies in breadth from 4 to 6 or 7 m., narrowing towards the W, where it is almost entirely shut in by hills, which, along the S side of the valley, rise by regular and gentle gradations above each other, while on the N side, they form one bold and continued range, separating the valley from the coast, and rising at one point, called the Peak of La Silla, or the Saddle, to a height of 1,350 toises, or 8,630 ft. above the level of the sea. This valley is watered by four small rivers; and its climate and soil are so delightful and fertile that a Spanish author has compared it with that of the terrestrial paradise, and the Anauco, and its three sister-streams with the four rivers which watered the garden of Eden. "In travelling from La Guayra to C.," says a very recent writer, "you come to a part of the road nearly level; as you trace its windings, bursts upon you at once the city of C.,—unexpected, magnificent, and inviting beyond the fanciful creations of romance. It is situated in a valley of which the expanse astonishes an eye not conversant with the interminable ravines of this country. You have the immense extent of the city distinctly exposed to your eye; and beyond you see the Guayra flowing in modest pride-I describe it in the dry period of the year—and the rich green of the maize raised on its banks for provender to animals, affords the liveliest contrast to surrounding dryness and barrenness." Close at the foot of the mountains, on the N side of the valley, stands the city, on ground which slopes regularly down to the Guayra, a small river which bounds it on the S, and which is everywhere fordable near the town, except after heavy rains, when it runs with great rapidity. The other three streams pass close to the town: namely, the Anauco, over which a handsome bridge is erected; the Caroata, which separates one part of the city from the rest, and over which is also erected a stone bridge; and the Catucho. All these four rivers, after administering to the domestic uses of the town, unite themselves into one channel, in which they flow through the valley of Chacao; and, at length, mingling their waters with those of the Tui, flow into the ocean, 36 m. E of Cape Codera. Of these three streams the Catucho is the most useful to the inhabitants, as from it they derive the principal supply of water for the public fountains, of which there are several; and also for the private houses, many of which are furnished with pipes and reservoirs. Besides its inclination to the S, the site of C. slopes also to the E, and is consequently wellcalculated for the health and convenience of a large town. After a heavy shower of rain, every street pours a muddy torrent into the Guayra or the Anauco; but in a few minutes all is again dry, and the whole town is suddenly rendered cleaner than it could be made by the utmost labour in any other not similarly situated. The city, which protrudes its buildings northward to the foot of the sierra, withdraws itself considerably from the Guayra on its S. It is in the form of a great square, every street cutting the transverse street at right angles, and thus forming a series of quadras or squares. There are about 15 streets thus formed from N to S; and as many from E to W. The more modern houses are seldom more than one story in height; but those built before the great earthquake have generally 3 or 4 stories. The streets are wretchedly paved; but no wheeled carriages are used in C. The streets are wide; and each house, enclosing a court with two floors of corridors, occupies an immense extent; no smoke or vapour from hearths or clouds, from artificial or natural causes, obstructs your view; and—while yet only approaching it-you riot in the contemplation of so splendid a monument of human industry and skill,—so rich a scene of human enjoyment and happiness. But much of this is illusion. On entering this city of presumed grandeur and wealth, "you are startled with the apprehension that you are in a city besieged by an enemy, or deserted from fear of some great convulsion of nature. Temple-bar or Charing-cross, at 6 o'clock on a Sunday morning, in summer, is more crowded and bustling than the busiest corner of C. at the busiest hour of the busiest day in the year. On looking around, you find that more than one-half of the houses in every street are uninhabited; many are in ruins; many shut up by the poverty and obstinacy of the proprietors, who cannot refit them, and will not sell them. With all its apparent superabundance of houses, houses are consequently enormously dear in C." To a certain extent, we may impute this desolate state of C. to earthquakes and civil wars. But the truth is, observes a visitor in 1826, "C. must have ever been a mushroom. Where in all time can you trace the industry, the commerce, and the wise polity of Spaniards in South America? And without these solid grounds of grandeur and wealth, what would their vaunted cities have been? The extortions of grandees and priests can adorn palaces and convents; but cities are not so formed, established, and adorned. C. ought, therefore, to be regarded as a monument of the former extent of those mighty agents of human misery, and its comparative desolation as a gratifying evidence of their fall." There are several squares in C.; but none of them worthy of notice, except the Plaza Mayor, or great square, where the market for fruits, vegetables, fish, and other smaller articles, is held. This square is 2,880 ft. above sea-level. The E side is principally occupied by the cathedral, a massive but irregular building; behind which, on the E and S sides, are the barracks. To the left of the cathedral is the archbishop's palace. Within pally by ranges of low shops, which, however convenient in a commercial view, entirely disfigure the whole. The principal fountain discharges itself in the middle of the N side. In the market held in this square may be seen fruits which we have been accustomed to consider as peculiar to very different climates, all brought from the distance of a few leagues. The banana, the pine-apple, and the sapadillo, are here mingled with the apple, the pear, and the chestnut; the potato and the plantain, fresh provisions which seem to belong to the temperate zones, and those kinds of fish which are peculiar to tropical seas, are here offered for sale on the same spot .- The public buildings of C. are chiefly those which are dedicated to the service of religion. The public functionaries occupy rented houses. The treasury is the only building which belongs to the government, and it is far from forming an ornament of the city. The cathedral is heavily built, and the interior construction badly planned; as, during the celebration of mass, a great proportion of the people which the church is capable of holding cannot see the priest. Its length is about 250 ft.; its breadth, 75 ft. It is supported by 24 pillars without beauty or proportion; but its brick steeple contains the only public clock in the city, and may compensate by its utility for what it wants in elegance. Next, in point of importance, and superior in the richness of its ornaments, was the parochial church of Alta Gracia, which was built chiefly at the expense of people of colour. It was by far the most splendid church in C., and did honour to the zeal of the contributors, if not to the taste of its architects, or of those who had the direction of its ornamenting. But this edifice was left a mass of ruins by the earthquake of 1812. The church of La Candelaria gives name to the quarter of the town in which it stands, and was built by the settlers from the Canaries, here called Islenos or islanders. Besides these there are 2 other parish-churches, St. Rosolio and St. Paul, and 3 hospitals, of which one is for lepers alone. The convents have been suppressed. The college is the chief public institution for education; and hither all the youth of C. of the better classes are sent for that purpose. The routine of education until recently was such as may be supposed to have been in Spain 200 years ago; a few Latin authors, the catechisms of the Church, and the Lives of Saints, being the books chiefly studied. Population.] The city of C. contained, according to the parish-certificates of 1802, 31,234 inhabitants. Depons, however, was of opinon that these certificates, in consequence of various inaccuracies, did not exhibit an accurate return, and he estimated the pop. of C. at 42,000. Before the great earthquake in 1812, it had risen to 50,000. [Lavaysse.] In that catastrophe it is estimated that nearly 12,000 of the inhabitants perished. Then followed an exterminating revolutionary war, and scarcely less destructive civil dissensions; and lastly, another dreadful earthquake in 1826. All these causes have contributed to reduce the pop. of C. to little more than one-half of what it was at the beginning of this century. It is a melange composed of whites, freed persons, slaves, and a few Indians; the first form nearly one-fourth of the whole, the slaves one-third, the Indians a twentieth, and the freed persons the remainder. The proper inhabitants of C., as of all the Colombian republics, are Creoles—a mixed race, descended from Spaniards and Negro or Indian women. The mixture seems to have degraded the blood of both. "Clothe a Creole with a little brief authority, and his blood boils with impatience to prove itself more allied to the White than the Black by the most capricious acts of ostentation, presumption, and vindictive cruelty. "All the commerce, the skill, and the handicraft industry of C., are in the hands of foreigners," writes a French author who visited C. in 1824. "The fashionable Caracanians are proprietors of immense tracts of country, on the partial and ill-proportioned revenue of which they doze out life; the lower classes live as donkey-drivers or dram-sellers. There is no society, no converse, no communication of intelligence or conviviality. They eat their meals in sensual sullenness, and then smoke cigars morning, noon, and night." This censure is no longer applicable, without considerable modification. Well-regulated schools exist in C., and several printing establishments. Commerce. Nearly all the commerce of Venezuela is in the hands of the merchants of C., who chiefly conduct it through the port of La Guayra, with which, however, the only communication until recently was by a mule-road of 5 Colombian leagues in length, which in crossing the coast-range of mountains attains an elevation at one point of 3,000 ft. above the plains of C., or 6,000 ft. above sea-level. Yet with good mules it requires but 3 hours to go from La Guayra to C., and only 2 to return. A carriage-road is now in course of formation. The chief articles of export are coffee, cocoa, hides, indigo, tobacco, cattle, cotton, mules and horses, and dibidivi. The following were the exports from La Guayra in the several undermentioned years, up to the 10th of August in each year: Cocoa. Cotton. Sugar. Indigo. Hides. Coffee. Quint. No. Quintals. Fanegas. Quint. Quint. The commerce of C. is further noticed in the articles LA GUAYRA and VENEZUELA. Climate.] The city of C. being situated at nearly 3,000 ft. above the level of the sea; is freed, in consequence of its elevation, from the excessive heats of the tropical regions. In winter, at 6 in the morning, Fahrenheit's therm. is generally at 58°; at 2 P. M. at 73°; and at 10 P. M. at 68°. In summer, at the same hours, it is at 72°, 79°, and 75°. Though delightfully cool in the mornings and evenings, the heat of noon is very great. Rain is abundant during April, May, and June. In the neighbourhood of C. are several pleasant villas and villages, surrounded by cultivated grounds, especially along the foot of the Silla. The climate of C. has been called a perpetual spring. "What indeed," says Humboldt, "can we imagine more delightful, than a temp. which, during the day, keeps between 16° and 28.8° of Reaumur; and at night between 12.8° and 14.4°; and which is equally favourable for the cultivation of the plantain, the orange-tree, the coffee-plant, the apricot, the apple, and corn? It is to be regretted, however, that this climate is generally inconstant and variable. The inhabitants complain of having several seasons in the course of the same day, and of the rapid transitions from one season to another. These oscillations are common in the temperate clime of Europe; but, under the torrid zone, Europeans themselves are so accustomed to the uniform action of exterior stimulus, that they suffer from a change of temperature of 6°. Moreover, these variations act on the human frame at C. more violently than could be supposed from the mere indications of the therm. In this narrow valley, the atmosphere is in some degree balanced between two winds: one coming from the sea-side, and known by the name of the wind of Catia, because it blows from Catia to the W of Cape Blanco; and the other from the E, or the inland country. The wind of Catia is apparently only a W wind, or more frequently a breeze, originating in the E and NE, and which, rushing with extreme impetuosity, engulfs itself in the quebrada or ravine of Tipe. It is loaded with moisture, which it deposits as its temp. decreases. The summit of the Silla is consequently wrapped in clouds when the Catia blows in the valley. This wind is dreaded by the inhabitants of C., causing headache to those persons whose nervous system is irritable. I have known some who, to shun its effects, shut themselves up in their houses, as people do in Italy when the sirocco blows. The wind of Petare coming from the E and SE, through the eastern extremity of the valley of the Guayra, brings from the mountains and the interior a drier air, which dissipates the clouds; and the summit of Silla then rises in all its beauty." The small extent of the valley in which C. stands, and the proximity of the high mountains of Avila and the Silla, impart, Humboldt adds, a stern and gloomy character to the scenery, CAR particularly in the months of November and December. The mornings are then very fine; and the two domes of the Silla and the craggy ridge of the Cerro d'Avila are seen relieved against a clear and serene sky. But towards evening the atmosphere thickens, and streams of vapour, clinging to the evergreen slopes of the mountains, seem to divide them into separate zones. These vapours are afterwards condensed into large fleecy clouds, which descend and creep along the soil, so that the traveller can scarcely imagine himself to be within the torrid zone. But this gloomy aspect of the sky is never seen in the midst of summer; and the nights of June and July are delicious. History.] The city of C. was founded by Diego de Losada, in 1567. The Spaniards, who were attracted thither by the reputation of the two gold-mines of Los Teques and Baruta, were not at that time masters of the whole valley, and they, therefore, fixed on a site near the road to the coast; but it is to be regretted, Humboldt thinks, that C. was not built further to the E, below the junction of the Anauco and the Guayra, on the spot near Chacao where the valley widens into an extensive plain. The bishopric of Coro was transferred to this city in 1636, and it was made an archbishopric in 1803. When Bolivar-himself a native of C.—appeared before the city at the head of a liberating army, on the 4th of August, 1813, he was received with great enthusiasm; and in the early reverses of the constitutionalists it suffered severely.
CARACAS, a group of three small uninhabited islands, near the coast of Venezuela, in Trigillo bay, and to the W of Cumana.
CARACAS, or CARAQUES BAY, an indentation of the coast of Ecuador, between Cape Passado on the N, and Charapolo point on the S, intersected by the parallel of 0° 30' S.
CARACATE, a town of Bolivia, in the intendancy and 55 m. SSE of La Paz.
